⑥ Merges and shapes the response
WireMock responds: acctNbr, acctStatus, avlBal, currCd
id VARCHAR PK · customer_id · account_number · status · nickname · created_at
id VARCHAR PK · account_id FK · available DECIMAL · current DECIMAL · currency
id VARCHAR PK · account_id FK · amount DECIMAL · description · posted_date · merchant_name
id VARCHAR PK · transaction_id FK · account_id FK · status · reason · estimated_resolution_date
WireMock :8099 · Prod: real Deluxe TRIPs endpoint
OWASP Top 10 — How the POC addresses common vulnerabilities
1. Built-in via Spring Boot / JPA — no extra coding needed
SQL Injection (A03): Spring Data JPA always uses prepared statements. You never write raw SQL,
so user input is never concatenated into a query string.
Sensitive Data Exposure (A02): Add @JsonIgnore on any PII field in the JPA entity
(e.g. raw account number, SSN). Spring will never include it in a JSON response.
Security Misconfiguration (A05): Spring Boot Actuator exposes only /health by default.
All other management endpoints are turned off unless you explicitly enable them.
2. Apollo Router config — add a few lines to router.yaml
Query Depth Limit (DoS protection): A client could craft a deeply nested query to overload resolvers.
Setting max-depth: 6 in the router stops this before it reaches Spring Boot.
Complexity Limit: Limits how many fields a single query can request at once (max-complexity: 150).
Turn off Introspection in production: Prevents attackers from exploring the full schema.
Apollo Studio still works because it uses the managed schema registry.
3. Spring Boot service layer — a small amount of code
Input Validation (A03): Use @Valid and @NotBlank on resolver arguments.
Throw a GraphQlException with a safe message — never expose internal details.
Rate Limiting (A07): Wrap the /graphql controller with a Resilience4j RateLimiter.
This limits how many requests a single IP can send per second.
// Spring Boot — input validation on a resolver
// Return a safe error — no stack trace to the client
Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) — Who can call what
Every request carries a JWT token from the bank's identity provider.
Roles inside the token decide what each caller is allowed to do.
Three roles used in this POC:
CUSTOMER — can read their own account and transactions only.
ADVISOR — can read any customer's account.
DISPUTE_AGENT — can open a dispute on a transaction.
The router checks the JWT signature against the bank JWKS endpoint.
Any request without a valid token is rejected with HTTP 401 before reaching Spring Boot.
Annotate each resolver method with which roles are allowed to call it.
Spring Security enforces this automatically — the method body never runs for unauthorised callers.
// Only CUSTOMER or ADVISOR can query an account
// Only DISPUTE_AGENT can open a dispute
Layer 3 — Inside the service — customers see only their own data
Even if a CUSTOMER has the right role, they should not see another customer's account.
A simple ownership check inside AccountService enforces this.
public Account findById(String id, UserPrincipal user) {
